questions.prompt: What's the recommended pattern for AWS API access from Pods running on EKS?

choices.text: Long-lived AWS access keys stored as Kubernetes Secrets.

IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA). Create an IAM role with the required AWS permissions; annotate the Kubernetes ServiceAccount with the IAM role's ARN; Pods using that ServiceAccount automatically get temporary AWS credentials via the SDK's standard credential chain. No long-lived AWS keys in Secrets, no rotation overhead, every Pod call to AWS is auditable in CloudTrail by the IAM role assumed. Each workload gets its own role with least-privilege permissions. This is dramatically more secure than the older pattern of storing AWS access keys as Kubernetes Secrets and mounting them — those keys don't rotate automatically, can be exposed via Secret reads (RBAC), and are visible to anyone who can describe the running Pod's environment. GKE has Workload Identity; AKS has Azure AD workload identity — each cloud provider has an equivalent. For any K8s workload calling cloud APIs, the federated-identity pattern is the modern standard.

questions.explanation: IRSA on EKS, Workload Identity on GKE, equivalent on AKS. Federated identity replaces long-lived cloud credentials — better security, easier rotation, better audit.

questions.prompt: A new operator's installation grants cluster-admin to its ServiceAccount. Should you install it as-is?

choices.text: Yes — the operator vendor knows best.

Be skeptical. cluster-admin lets the operator do anything in the cluster — read all Secrets across all namespaces, modify any resource, install other operators, basically take over. That's almost never what the operator actually needs to do its job. Before installing, review what specific permissions are required: usually it needs to manage its own CRDs (the resources it watches and creates), some related resources (Pods, Services, ConfigMaps that it creates from those CRDs), and observability things (Events, metrics). A custom ClusterRole with just these specific permissions, bound to the operator's ServiceAccount, achieves the same functionality with much smaller blast radius. If the operator is compromised, the damage is contained to its actual scope. Some vendors ship overly-broad permissions out of laziness or to avoid support tickets — push back when you can. For trusted vendors, accept slightly looser permissions if they justify it; for operators you're evaluating, this is a quality signal. Trust-but-verify is the right posture; cluster-admin in production should be an emergency break-glass, not a routine grant.

questions.explanation: cluster-admin in production is almost always too broad. Trust-but-verify operator permissions; constrain to actual needs. The blast radius of a compromised operator with cluster-admin is everything.
